section.title: A Song of Trust
section.sectionRationale: This song of Judah continues the Isaiah apocalypse, chapters 24 to 27, turning from the LORD's cosmic judgment and banquet of chapter 25 to a sustained hymn about trusting Him as the Rock eternal. It reaches one of the Old Testament's clearest statements of bodily resurrection, your dead will live, their bodies will rise, before calling God's people to hide briefly until His wrath has passed.
section.thesis: The LORD keeps in perfect peace all who trust in Him as the Rock eternal, and though His people may labor in distress and seem to accomplish nothing lasting, He promises that their dead will live and their bodies will rise, so they can safely hide until His wrath against the earth has passed.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the strong city whose walls are salvation, and trust in the eternal Rock; the level path of the righteous who wait for the LORD's judgments; peace established and other lords renounced; labor pains that produced nothing lasting; and the resurrection promise followed by a call to hide until the wrath passes.

units.label: A strong city, trust in the eternal Rock

verses.text: In that day this song will be sung in the land of Judah: We have a strong city; salvation is established as its walls and ramparts.
Open the gates so a righteous nation may enter, one that remains faithful.
You will keep in perfect {{shalom|peace}} the steadfast of mind, because he trusts in You.
Trust in the LORD forever, because GOD the LORD is the {{tsurOlamim|Rock eternal}}.
For He has humbled those who dwell on high; He lays the lofty city low. He brings it down to the ground; He casts it into the dust.
Feet trample it down, the feet of the oppressed, the steps of the poor.

units.label: The level path of the righteous

verses.text: The path of the righteous is level; You clear a straight path for the upright.
Yes, we wait for You, O LORD; we walk in the path of Your judgments. Your name and renown are the desire of our souls.
My soul longs for You in the night; indeed, my spirit seeks You at dawn. For when Your judgments come upon the earth, the people of the world learn righteousness.
Though grace is shown to the wicked man, he does not learn righteousness. In the land of righteousness he acts unjustly and fails to see the majesty of the LORD.
O LORD, Your hand is upraised, but they do not see it. They will see Your zeal for Your people and be put to shame. The fire set for Your enemies will consume them!

units.label: Peace established, other lords renounced
units.note: Your name alone do we confess

verses.text: O LORD, You will establish peace for us. For all that we have accomplished, You have done for us.
O LORD our God, other lords besides You have had dominion, but Your name alone do we confess.
The dead will not live; the departed spirits will not rise. Therefore You have punished and destroyed them; You have wiped out all memory of them.
You have enlarged the nation, O LORD; You have enlarged the nation. You have gained glory for Yourself; You have extended all the borders of the land.

units.label: Labor that gave birth to wind

verses.text: O LORD, they sought You in their distress; when You disciplined them, they poured out a quiet prayer.
As a woman with child about to give birth writhes and cries out in pain, so were we in Your presence, O LORD.
We were with child; we writhed in pain; but we gave birth to wind. We have given no salvation to the earth, nor brought any life into the world.

units.label: Your dead will live, hide until the wrath passes

verses.text: Your dead will live; their bodies will rise. Awake and sing, you who dwell in the dust! For your dew is like the dew of the morning, and the earth will bring forth her dead.
Go, my people, enter your rooms and shut your doors behind you. Hide yourselves a little while until the wrath has passed.
For behold, the LORD is coming out of His dwelling to punish the inhabitants of the earth for their iniquity. The earth will reveal her bloodshed and will no longer conceal her slain.

shalom.gloss: Peace, doubled for emphasis, meaning something like perfect or complete peace. This wholeness of peace is promised specifically to the one whose mind is stayed on the LORD, tying inward stability directly to the object of one's trust rather than to circumstances.

tsurOlamim.gloss: The Rock of ages, or eternal Rock, a title emphasizing the LORD's unchanging, everlasting reliability as a foundation for trust. Unlike any lofty city that can be humbled and cast into dust, this Rock never falls and never fails, the very security the New Testament identifies with Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:4.

basic.context: This chapter is a song meant to be sung by God's people, celebrating a strong city whose true walls are salvation itself rather than stone. It moves through trust in the Rock eternal, patient waiting for God's judgments, honest confession that human effort alone produces nothing lasting, and climaxes in one of the clearest Old Testament promises of bodily resurrection: your dead will live, their bodies will rise.
basic.hermeneutics: This song describes both the settled trust available to God's people now and a resurrection hope that reaches beyond any single historical event. Verse 19's resurrection promise is a genuine, if compressed, seed of what the New Testament makes explicit and central, while verse 14's denial of resurrection to the wicked, by contrast, sharpens the difference between those who trust the LORD and those who set themselves against Him.
basic.originalLanguages: The doubled Hebrew shalom shalom promises a peace that is whole and complete to the one whose mind rests on the LORD, not merely calm circumstances. That trust is anchored in the tsur olamim, the Rock of ages, a title of everlasting, unshakable reliability that Paul later applies to Christ Himself, 1 Corinthians 10:4.
basic.typology: The Rock eternal, unshakable while every lofty human city eventually falls, anticipates Christ, the spiritual Rock that never fails His people. The resurrection promise, your dead will live, their bodies will rise, anticipates and is fulfilled in Christ's own bodily resurrection and the future resurrection of all who trust in Him.
basic.christ: This chapter's Rock eternal, on whom the steadfast mind rests in perfect peace, is fulfilled in Christ, the spiritual Rock that followed Israel, 1 Corinthians 10:4, and the one true foundation who never gives way. Its resurrection promise, your dead will live, their bodies will rise, finds its guarantee and firstfruits in Christ's own resurrection, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23, so that all who are united to Him share the same certain hope.
basic.god: God keeps in perfect peace those whose minds are fixed on Him, not because their circumstances are easy but because He Himself is an unshakable Rock. He disciplines His people in distress, honestly measures the emptiness of purely human striving, and yet promises resurrection life to the dead who are His, while inviting His people to hide safely until His righteous wrath against the earth's bloodshed has passed.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 10:4 identifies Christ as the spiritual Rock. 1 Corinthians 15:20-23 grounds the resurrection hope of believers in Christ's own resurrection as firstfruits. Daniel 12:2 offers a similarly early, explicit resurrection promise. Revelation 6:9-11 pictures God's people sheltered briefly while His judgment on the earth's bloodshed plays out, echoing verses 20 to 21 closely.
basic.oneStory: This song moves believers from trusting the LORD as an unshakable Rock, through honest acknowledgment that human effort alone accomplishes nothing lasting, to the astonishing promise that the dead will live again. It anticipates the whole gospel hope secured in Christ, our Rock and our resurrection, in whom trusting hearts are kept in perfect peace both now and forever.

context.literary: A song, explicitly introduced as such in verse 1, moving through contrasting images: a strong city versus a humbled lofty one, a level path versus futile labor pains, and finally death versus resurrected life, structured to build toward its climactic promise in verse 19.
context.historical: Likely composed for use by the returned or restored community of Judah, reflecting on a history in which their own efforts, however earnest, failed to produce lasting deliverance, they writhed in labor but gave birth to wind, verse 18, preparing them to receive by faith what only God's own resurrection power could accomplish.
context.canonical: Contains one of the clearest and earliest explicit affirmations of bodily resurrection in the Old Testament, alongside Daniel 12:2 and Job 19:25-27, forming a crucial thread the New Testament picks up and fulfills in the resurrection of Christ and the future resurrection of His people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach God's people to root their peace and trust in the LORD as an unshakable Rock rather than in human effort or circumstance, and to anchor their ultimate hope not in what they can accomplish but in the resurrection life only God can give.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This song describes the posture of trust and hope God's people are invited to share; it is less a prescription of specific actions than an invitation to a settled confidence and a resurrection hope, applicable in any age of waiting and apparent futility.

debates.question: Does the resurrection promise in verse 19, your dead will live, their bodies will rise, refer to Israel's national restoration, individual bodily resurrection, or both?

views.label: A metaphor for Israel's national revival
views.case: Some read the dead rising as figurative language for the nation's restoration from exile or defeat, similar to the dry bones vision in Ezekiel 37, a corporate rather than individual resurrection.

views.label: A genuine promise of individual bodily resurrection
views.case: The vivid, physical language, bodies will rise, awake and sing, you who dwell in the dust, and its contrast with verse 14's denial of resurrection to the wicked, suggests a real individual hope, one the New Testament later confirms and fulfills explicitly in Christ.

debates.bearing: The two readings need not be mutually exclusive; national restoration imagery often carries and points toward a genuine individual resurrection hope, and the New Testament's clear teaching on bodily resurrection, grounded in Christ's own rising, confirms that this verse's ultimate meaning includes real, personal, bodily resurrection.

study.originalLanguages: The doubled shalom shalom promises a peace that is whole, not partial, granted specifically to the mind fixed on the LORD rather than on circumstances. This trust rests on the tsur olamim, the Rock of ages, a title of everlasting reliability the New Testament directly applies to Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:4, tying this ancient song's confidence to the person of Jesus.

typology.type: The Rock eternal, unshaken while every lofty city falls
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the spiritual Rock that followed Israel and never fails those who trust in Him, 1 Corinthians 10:4.

typology.type: Your dead will live, their bodies will rise
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own bodily resurrection as firstfruits, guaranteeing the future resurrection of all who are united to Him, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23.

study.christ: The chapter's Rock eternal, the sure foundation for the mind kept in perfect peace, is the same Rock Paul identifies as Christ, the spiritual Rock that followed Israel, 1 Corinthians 10:4. Its resurrection promise, your dead will live, their bodies will rise, is not fulfilled by any human effort, the labor that produced only wind, verse 18, but by Christ's own resurrection, the firstfruits guaranteeing that everyone united to Him will likewise rise, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23. The song's whole logic, trust rather than self-effort, culminating in resurrection hope, is the logic of the gospel itself.
study.god: God grants perfect peace to minds fixed on Him, a peace rooted in His own unchanging, everlasting nature as the Rock eternal, not in favorable circumstances. He is honest about the futility of human effort apart from Him and just in disciplining His people, yet His ultimate purpose is resurrection life for His own, even while His righteous wrath against the earth's accumulated bloodshed must still be fully revealed and judged.

crossReferences.note: Identifies Christ as the spiritual Rock, the fulfillment of this chapter's Rock eternal.
Grounds believers' resurrection hope in Christ's own resurrection as firstfruits, fulfilling this chapter's promise that the dead will live.
A similarly early and explicit Old Testament affirmation of bodily resurrection, some to everlasting life and some to shame.
Pictures God's people sheltered while awaiting the full outworking of God's judgment on the earth's bloodshed, echoing verses 20 to 21.

study.oneStory: This song moves believers from resting their peace in the LORD as an unshakable Rock, through honest recognition that human striving alone yields nothing lasting, to the astonishing hope that the dead will live again. It anticipates the whole gospel story, fulfilled in Christ, our Rock and our resurrection, in whom every mind that trusts Him is kept in perfect peace both now and in the age to come.

sermon.title: Live for Your Greatest Desire: Jesus
sermon.why: Piper preaches on Isaiah 26:8, your name and renown are the desire of our souls, unpacking how God-centered desire and trust in the LORD, the very themes running through this whole song of trust, find their full satisfaction in Jesus.

quiz.q: What does verse 3 promise to the one whose mind is steadfast?

options.text: Perfect peace, because he trusts in the LORD.
options.note: Verse 3 ties this perfect peace directly to trust in the LORD, not to circumstances.

options.text: Immediate wealth and political power.
options.note: Not promised in this verse; the promise is inward peace, not wealth or power.

options.text: Freedom from all future hardship.
options.note: The chapter elsewhere describes ongoing distress and discipline, so this overstates the promise.

options.text: A guaranteed long earthly life.
options.note: Not the specific promise of this verse, which concerns inward peace rooted in trust, not lifespan.

quiz.q: What title for God appears in verse 4, emphasizing His reliability?

options.text: The Rock eternal, or Rock of ages.
options.note: Verse 4 names God the LORD as the Rock eternal, tsur olamim.

options.text: The Shepherd of Israel.
options.note: A true biblical title elsewhere, but not the specific title used in this verse.

options.text: The King of kings.
options.note: A true biblical title, but not the specific phrase used in verse 4, which is Rock eternal.

options.text: The Judge of all the earth.
options.note: Used elsewhere in Scripture, but not the title given in this particular verse.

quiz.q: What honest confession do the people make in verse 18 about their own efforts?

options.text: They writhed in labor pains but gave birth only to wind, accomplishing no lasting salvation.
options.note: Verse 18 states this directly, a stark admission of human effort's futility apart from God.

options.text: Their efforts fully saved the nation without God's help.
options.note: The opposite; the confession is that their own efforts produced nothing lasting.

options.text: They successfully brought new life and salvation to the whole earth.
options.note: The text explicitly denies this outcome; no salvation or life resulted from their labor.

options.text: They gave up trying entirely and did nothing at all.
options.note: The image is of real, painful labor and effort, not passivity, that still produced nothing lasting.

quiz.q: What resurrection promise appears in verse 19?

options.text: Your dead will live; their bodies will rise, and the earth will bring forth her dead.
options.note: Verse 19 states this resurrection hope explicitly and vividly.

options.text: The dead will remain in the ground permanently with no hope of rising.
options.note: This describes verse 14's statement about the wicked, the opposite of verse 19's promise to God's own.

options.text: Only a select few individuals will ever be resurrected.
options.note: The verse speaks broadly of the dead among God's people rising, not a narrowly restricted few.

options.text: Resurrection will occur but only in a purely symbolic, non-bodily sense.
options.note: The language, bodies will rise, dwell in the dust, strongly suggests a genuinely bodily hope, not merely symbolic renewal.

quiz.q: What are God's people told to do in verse 20, right after the resurrection promise?

options.text: Enter their rooms and hide briefly until the LORD's wrath against the earth has passed.
options.note: Verse 20 gives this instruction directly, immediately following the resurrection hope of verse 19.

options.text: Go out and confront the coming wrath directly.
options.note: The opposite instruction is given; they are told to hide, not confront the wrath directly.

options.text: Abandon their homes permanently and flee the land.
options.note: The instruction is to hide briefly, a little while, not to abandon the land permanently.

options.text: Gather publicly to celebrate before the wrath even begins.
options.note: The instruction is specifically to hide and shut their doors, not to gather in public celebration at this point.

questions: The steadfast mind is kept in perfect peace because it trusts in the LORD, the Rock eternal. What is currently competing with God to be the rock you actually trust for your peace?

The people honestly confess that their own labor produced no lasting salvation, only wind. Where have you been striving hard at something that, if you are honest, cannot ultimately save or fulfill you?
Verse 19 gives one of the Bible's clearest Old Testament promises of resurrection. How does the certainty of your own future bodily resurrection in Christ change how you view suffering or loss today?

God's people are told to hide briefly until His wrath has passed, trusting His timing rather than facing it themselves. Where do you need to trust God's protection and timing rather than trying to manage a threatening situation on your own?
The song moves from confessing other lords have ruled over them to declaring, Your name alone do we confess. What competing loyalties or lesser lords might God be calling you to renounce this week?

translationNotes.note: Perfect peace renders a doubled Hebrew word, shalom shalom, an intensifying construction suggesting completeness rather than a partial or occasional peace.
This verse's denial of resurrection applies specifically to oppressive powers or the wicked, contrasted deliberately with the resurrection hope promised to God's own people in verse 19.
Your dew is like the dew of the morning uses a vivid image of refreshment and renewal after death, likening resurrection to dew that revives parched ground.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 26:1-21. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; no em dashes were present in the source text for this chapter.
